diff_summary: apps/jobs/src/jobs/commission-run.ts (renames the monthly run to `commission.monthly`, declares cron `0 2 1 * *` + onFailure `alert-only` + maxAttempts 3, wraps the prior-month close in a 3-attempt in-handler retry loop that on exhaustion audits `commission.run.failed`, error-logs, and rethrows), apps/jobs/src/registry.ts (adds optional `Job.maxAttempts` + `JobDescriptor.maxAttempts` defaulting to 1 in schedule()), plus the matching job + registry tests. Registers the monthly commission run under the P3-E06 jobs framework.
ac_coverage: AC1 (registered as `commission.monthly` with cron `0 2 1 * *`): the DESCRIPTOR is correct (name + cron + onFailure + maxAttempts surfaced, tested) but the cron is NOT honored — startScheduler fires purely off intervalMs (MONTHLY_MS = 30 days) and no cron parser exists in apps/jobs, so the real cadence is every-30-days, not 02:00-on-the-1st. AC2 (failures retried; max 3 attempts before alert): implemented via an in-handler 3-attempt loop + audit/log/rethrow on exhaustion, and tested (retry-then-succeed, exhaust-then-alert) — but the failure-path audit write is unguarded and the framework does not itself enforce maxAttempts. Idempotency (per-month unique index) HOLDS under framework-driven re-fires.

findings.title: AC1 cron `0 2 1 * *` is declared but never honored — the framework schedules `commission.monthly` off a 30-day setInterval, so the calendar-month close drifts and is not pinned to 02:00 on the 1st
findings.detail: This story registers the run as `commission.monthly` with cron `0 2 1 * *` (commission-run.ts:61-63) and the descriptor faithfully surfaces it (registry.ts:59 schedule()). But the framework's only scheduling primitive is `setInterval(job.intervalMs)` in startScheduler (runner.ts:149-160) — the `cron` field is never parsed, and apps/jobs has NO cron dependency (verified: package.json lists only drizzle-orm + workspace pkgs; grep finds no node-cron/croner/cron-parser). The job's intervalMs is MONTHLY_MS = 30*24*60*60*1000 (commission-run.ts:7,62). A fixed 30-day interval is not a calendar month: anchored to worker boot, it slides ~0.44 day earlier each cycle relative to the calendar and never re-aligns to the 1st. Each fire closes priorMonthPeriod(now), the calendar month before the firing instant, so the month a run closes depends on boot time + drift: a fire can land on, e.g., the 27th and close the prior month while the current month is still accruing, and two fires can land in one calendar month (the 2nd is an idempotent no-op — wasted) while a payout that the spec wants promptly at 02:00 on the 1st can slip days late. Idempotency prevents DOUBLE-closing a month but does not guarantee each month is closed once AND on time. The job test (commission-run.test.ts:80-89) only asserts `job.cron === '0 2 1 * *'` and `intervalMs > 0`; it never asserts the cron is actually honored, so the declared-vs-actual gap is untested. This is the framework-wide intervalMs-not-cron limitation, but it bites hardest here because the cadence primitive (30 days) is the wrong unit for a calendar-month boundary, and this is the story that asserts the cron contract.
findings.fix: Framework-level decision (already raised on P3-E01-S03 as a blocker). Options: (a) make startScheduler cron-aware (add a cron-parser dep and schedule the next `0 2 1 * *` boundary instead of a fixed interval); or (b) keep intervalMs but change the cadence to be self-correcting — fire daily at 02:00 and have runOnce no-op unless the prior calendar month is still unclosed (the partial unique index already makes extra fires safe), which closes each month promptly on the 1st without a cron parser. A 30-day interval is the wrong primitive for a once-a-calendar-month close regardless of which is chosen.

findings.title: AC2 failure alert is lost when the DB is the cause of failure — the `commission.run.failed` audit insert is itself an unguarded DB write that throws before the error-log + intended rethrow
findings.detail: On exhausting all 3 attempts the handler does `await audit(db, { action: 'commission.run.failed', ... })` (commission-run.ts:84-89), THEN `log.error(...)` (90-93), THEN `throw lastError` (94). `audit()` is a bare `db.insert(auditOutbox)...returning()` that rejects on any DB error (packages/db/src/audit.ts:31-42). The most common real cause of a commission run failing all 3 immediate attempts is the DB being unavailable/erroring — in exactly that case this audit insert ALSO throws. Consequences: (1) the `commission.run.failed` audit row — the AC2 alert on the audit side — is never written; (2) the 'commission run failed after max attempts' error line is never logged; (3) the value that propagates to the framework's runJob is the audit-insert's error, not `lastError`, so job_runs.error and the Sentry capture record a misleading message (e.g. an insert/connection error) rather than the real persistent-failure cause. runJob still records a failed run + captures *an* exception, so the failure isn't fully silent, but the AC2 'raise an alert' audit + log the story promises are skipped on the realistic DB-down path. The exhaustion test (commission-run.test.ts:107-130) only injects failure via a throwing clock with a HEALTHY db, so the audit write always succeeds there and this branch is never exercised.
findings.fix: Guard the failure-path side effects so the original error always surfaces: emit the error log FIRST, wrap the `audit(...)` in its own try/catch that logs (but does not rethrow) an audit-write failure, and always `throw lastError instanceof Error ? lastError : new Error(message)` at the end — mirroring the same defensive pattern runner.ts already uses around its terminal job_runs write (runner.ts:114-124). Add a test that fails the audit write (or the underlying db) to confirm the original `lastError` is what propagates.

findings.title: AC2 retries are 3 immediate back-to-back attempts with no backoff, and `maxAttempts` is descriptor-only (the framework does not enforce it), so all 3 attempts collapse into one job_runs row
findings.detail: The retry loop (commission-run.ts:66-79) runs the 3 attempts synchronously in a tight for-loop with zero delay between them. For a transient fault (DB blip, lock timeout) three retries within milliseconds are unlikely to span the outage, so in practice the job often goes straight to the alert — the retry adds little resilience versus sibling jobs that just `retry-next-tick`. Separately, `maxAttempts` declared on the Job/descriptor (registry.ts:31,41,62) is purely observability: runJob (runner.ts:68-128) invokes `job.run()` exactly once and never reads maxAttempts, so the actual retrying lives entirely inside the handler and all 3 attempts are recorded as a SINGLE job_runs row — per-attempt failures appear only in logs (`commission.run.attempt_failed`), not in job_runs. Both behaviours are internally consistent with the doc comments and arguably acceptable for a once-a-month one-shot, but the spec wording 'failures retried; max 3 attempts before alert' is ambiguous about backoff and about whether attempts should be observable as distinct runs.
findings.fix: Confirm intended retry semantics. If transient-fault resilience matters, add a short backoff between attempts (e.g. the BACKOFF_MS pattern used by sms-retry) and/or have the framework record each attempt. If the immediate-3x + alert-only-one-shot model is intended (reasonable for a monthly close that self-heals next tick), document that the retries are best-effort-immediate and that maxAttempts is observability metadata only.
